title: Install via Unity Hub & Tour the Editor
html:
Why Unity Hub exists

Unity ships as two things: the Hub (a launcher that manages installs and projects) and the Editor (the actual engine). You almost never install the Editor directly — the Hub installs and version-manages it for you, which matters because real teams pin a project to one exact Editor version to avoid subtle behavior differences.

Install steps

  1. Download Unity Hub from unity.com/download and install it. Sign in with a free Personal license (fine for learning and small revenue).
  2. In the Hub, go to the Installs tab and click Install Editor. Pick the latest LTS (Long-Term Support) version — LTS gets two years of patches and is what you ship on.
  3. In the module selection screen, add the build support you need now: Windows/Mac Build Support is included for your OS; add the Mac/Windows Build Support (Mono) for the other desktop, and consider WebGL. You can add modules later via the gear menu.
LTS, not Tech Stream.
The newest "Tech Stream" builds add features but also bugs. For a course and for shipping, choose the highest LTS number available.

Create your first project

Go to the Projects tab → New project. Choose a template: 3D (Built-in Render Pipeline) is the simplest place to start. Name it RollABall, pick a folder, and create. The first open is slow — Unity is importing packages and compiling.

The five views you live in

ViewWhat it is
SceneThe 3D/2D editing viewport. You arrange objects here. Not the running game.
GameWhat the player sees through the active Camera when you press Play.
HierarchyA list of every GameObject in the open scene, with parent/child nesting.
InspectorThe properties of whatever is selected — its components and their values.
ProjectYour asset files on disk (scripts, models, prefabs, scenes).

Navigating the Scene view

This is muscle memory you must build now:

  • Orbit: hold Alt (Option on Mac) + left-drag.
  • Pan: middle-mouse drag, or Alt + middle-drag.
  • Zoom: scroll wheel.
  • Flythrough: hold right-mouse and use W A S D like an FPS; Q/E for down/up.
  • Frame selected: select an object and press F to snap the camera to it. This is the single most useful shortcut.

The transform tools are on the toolbar and bound to Q (hand/pan), W (move), E (rotate), R (scale), T (rect).

Scene Camera ≠ Main Camera.
Moving around in the Scene view does not move the camera the player sees. The player's view comes from the Main Camera GameObject. Beginners panic when Play looks nothing like the Scene view — that's why.

Adding objects

Right-click in the Hierarchy → 3D Object → Plane for a ground. Right-click → 3D Object → Sphere and → Cube for a couple of primitives. Select each and use the Move (W) gizmo to spread them out. Press F to frame them.

Position the player camera

Select Main Camera in the Hierarchy. A small preview shows its view. Use GameObject → Align With View to snap the Main Camera to your current Scene view angle — a fast way to set up a decent starting shot. Then press Play (the triangle at top center) to enter Play mode; the Game view shows your scene through that camera. Press Play again to stop.

Save the scene.
Press Ctrl/Cmd+S. Scenes are files; if you never save, closing Unity loses your layout. Save it as Main inside an Assets/Scenes folder.
🎮 Build This: First Scene

A navigable scene with primitives and a positioned camera.

  1. Create a 3D project in the Hub on the latest LTS.
  2. Add a Plane, a Sphere, and a Cube; spread them out with the Move tool.
  3. Practice orbit (Alt-drag), flythrough (right-mouse + WASD), and Frame Selected (F).
  4. Select Main Camera, frame a nice angle in Scene view, then GameObject → Align With View.
  5. Press Play to confirm the Game view matches; save the scene as Main.

title: GameObjects, Components & the Inspector
html:
The core idea: composition over inheritance

A GameObject by itself is almost nothing — an empty container with a name and a Transform. All behavior and appearance comes from Components you attach to it. A camera is a GameObject + Camera component. A visible cube is a GameObject + Mesh Filter + Mesh Renderer. You compose objects from parts rather than inheriting from a deep class tree. This is the single most important mental model in Unity.

Every GameObject has exactly one Transform.
You can't remove it. Position, rotation, and scale live there, and child objects inherit their parent's transform — move the parent, the children move with it.

Reading the Inspector

Select a GameObject and the Inspector stacks its components top to bottom. Each component is a collapsible block with a checkbox to enable/disable it. The header row has the GameObject's name, an active checkbox, a Tag, and a Layer. Tags label objects for code lookups ("Player", "Enemy"); Layers control physics and rendering grouping.

Anatomy of a visible mesh

Create a 3D Object → Sphere and look at its Inspector. You'll see:

ComponentJob
TransformWhere/how it sits in space.
Mesh FilterHolds the geometry (the sphere mesh).
Mesh RendererDraws the mesh using one or more Materials.
Sphere ColliderThe physics shape (added automatically on primitives).

Build a lit scene with no code

  1. Create a 3D Object → Plane. In its Transform set Scale to (2, 1, 2) so it's a roomy floor. Leave Position at (0,0,0).
  2. Create a 3D Object → Sphere. Set Position to (0, 0.5, 0) so it rests on the plane (the sphere's radius is 0.5).
  3. A new 3D scene already has a Directional Light in the Hierarchy. Select it. The Transform's rotation is what matters for a directional light — position is irrelevant; it acts like the sun, lighting everything from an angle. Set rotation to about (50, -30, 0).

Make a material and tune it

A plain primitive uses a default gray material. Create your own: in the Project window, right-click → Create → Material, name it BallMat. Select it; in the Inspector set Base Map / Albedo to a color, and drag the Metallic and Smoothness sliders to taste. Then drag the material from Project onto the Sphere in the Scene or Hierarchy to assign it.

Drag-and-drop is wiring.
Assigning materials, dropping objects into component slots, parenting in the Hierarchy — most non-code setup in Unity is dragging one thing onto another. Get comfortable with it.

Parenting and Reset

Drag the Sphere onto an empty GameObject in the Hierarchy to make it a child; now the child's Transform values are relative to the parent. To zero a transform, click the component's context menu (the three dots) → Reset. To add any component, use the Add Component button at the bottom of the Inspector and search by name.

Light settings can wash out everything.
If your scene is blindingly bright or pitch black, check the Directional Light's Intensity and the ambient lighting in Window → Rendering → Lighting. A too-high intensity or a stray skybox setting is the usual culprit.
🎮 Build This: Lit Studio Scene

A clean ground + sphere lit by a directional light, configured entirely in the Inspector.

  1. Add a Plane scaled to (2,1,2) as the floor.
  2. Add a Sphere at (0, 0.5, 0).
  3. Create a Material, color it, set Metallic/Smoothness, and drag it onto the sphere.
  4. Select the Directional Light and rotate it (~50, -30, 0) so the sphere casts a shadow.
  5. Adjust light Intensity until the shading reads well; save the scene.

title: C# Scripting: MonoBehaviour, Update & [SerializeField]
html:
Scripts are components too

When you write a C# class that inherits from MonoBehaviour, Unity lets you attach it to a GameObject as a component. That's the bridge between your code and the engine. Unity calls special methods on your class at the right times — you don't run main(), you fill in event methods and the engine invokes them.

Create a script

In the Project window, right-click → Create → C# Script (or use Add Component → New Script on the GameObject). Name it PlayerMovement. Important: the filename must match the class name exactly, or Unity refuses to attach it. Double-click to open it in your editor (Visual Studio, VS Code, or Rider).

PlayerMovement.cs
using UnityEngine; public class PlayerMovement : MonoBehaviour { // Shows in the Inspector but stays private to other code [SerializeField] private float speed = 5f; // Called once before the first frame void Start() { Debug.Log("Player ready"); } // Called every frame void Update() { float h = Input.GetAxisRaw("Horizontal"); // A/D or arrows: -1..1 float v = Input.GetAxisRaw("Vertical"); // W/S or arrows Vector3 move = new Vector3(h, 0f, v); // dt keeps speed the same on any framerate transform.Translate(move * speed * Time.deltaTime); } }

The lifecycle methods that matter

MethodWhen it runs
Awake()When the object loads — before any Start. Wire up references here.
Start()Once, before the first Update. Safe to access other objects' Awake results.
Update()Every rendered frame. Input and most game logic.
FixedUpdate()On the fixed physics timestep. Physics/Rigidbody code goes here (next lesson).

Why [SerializeField] instead of public

You want speed tunable in the Inspector without recompiling, but you don't want other scripts reaching in and changing it. public exposes it to the Inspector and all code. [SerializeField] private exposes it to the Inspector only — it keeps your encapsulation clean. This is the professional default.

Always scale movement by Time.deltaTime.
Update runs more times per second on a faster machine. Multiplying by deltaTime (seconds since last frame) makes speed mean "units per second" regardless of framerate.

Build Roll-a-Ball movement

  1. Open your scene with the Plane and Sphere. Rename the Sphere to Player.
  2. Attach PlayerMovement to the Player (drag the script onto it, or Add Component).
  3. In the Inspector you'll now see a Speed field with the value 5. Change it to 8 and notice you didn't touch code.
  4. Press Play and drive with the arrow keys / WASD.
Edits during Play don't persist.
Any value you tweak in the Inspector while in Play mode resets the instant you stop. Tune in Play to find good numbers, then re-enter them with Play stopped. Unity tints the editor when you're in Play mode as a reminder.
Use Debug.Log liberally.
Output appears in the Console window. It's your fastest debugging tool before you reach for breakpoints. Debug.Log($"h={h} v={v}"); with string interpolation shows live values each frame.
🎮 Build This: Roll-a-Ball (movement)

A sphere you drive across a plane at a serialized speed.

  1. Reuse the Plane + Sphere scene; rename the sphere to Player.
  2. Create PlayerMovement with a [SerializeField] float speed.
  3. In Update, read Horizontal/Vertical, build a Vector3, and Translate by speed * Time.deltaTime.
  4. Attach it to Player; press Play and steer with the arrow keys.
  5. Tune Speed in the Inspector until it feels right.

title: Physics: Rigidbody, Colliders & Triggers
html:
Two pieces: Rigidbody and Collider

Real physics needs two components working together. A Collider defines the shape used for contact tests. A Rigidbody tells the physics engine to simulate the object — apply gravity, respond to forces, resolve collisions. A collider without a Rigidbody is a static, immovable wall; add a Rigidbody and it falls and bounces.

Move Rigidbodies with forces, not the Transform.
If you teleport a physics object via transform.position every frame, you fight the simulation and get jitter and tunneling. Drive it with AddForce / velocity inside FixedUpdate instead.

Rebuild Roll-a-Ball on physics

  1. Select the Player sphere → Add Component → Rigidbody. It now has mass, drag, and gravity. Press Play and it falls onto the plane and rolls.
  2. Replace the Translate-based movement with force-based movement in FixedUpdate.
BallController.cs
using UnityEngine; [RequireComponent(typeof(Rigidbody))] public class BallController : MonoBehaviour { [SerializeField] private float force = 12f; private Rigidbody rb; private Vector3 input; private int score; void Awake() => rb = GetComponent<Rigidbody>(); void Update() { // Read input every frame (cheap, responsive) float h = Input.GetAxisRaw("Horizontal"); float v = Input.GetAxisRaw("Vertical"); input = new Vector3(h, 0f, v); } void FixedUpdate() { // Apply physics on the fixed timestep rb.AddForce(input * force); } // Fires when this collider enters a TRIGGER collider void OnTriggerEnter(Collider other) { if (other.CompareTag("Pickup")) { other.gameObject.SetActive(false); // hide it score++; Debug.Log($"Score: {score}"); } } }

Colliders vs. triggers

A collider has an Is Trigger checkbox. Unchecked (the default), it's solid — objects physically stop against it and you get OnCollisionEnter. Checked, it's a ghost — objects pass through it and you get OnTriggerEnter. Pickups, checkpoints, and damage zones are triggers; walls and floors are solid colliders.

EventFires whenParam
OnCollisionEnterTwo solid colliders touchCollision
OnTriggerEnterSomething enters a triggerCollider
OnTriggerExitSomething leaves a triggerCollider
The collision matrix rule.
For any of these events to fire, at least one of the two objects must have a Rigidbody, and both need Colliders. Two static colliders with no Rigidbody never report a collision. This is the #1 reason "my OnTrigger isn't firing."

Make pickups

  1. Create a small Cube or Capsule; position it on the plane. Set its scale small.
  2. In the Inspector, check Is Trigger on its collider.
  3. Create a Tag called Pickup (Tag dropdown → Add Tag, then assign it).
  4. Duplicate it (Ctrl/Cmd+D) several times and spread them around.

Now roll the ball through them — each one disappears and the Console prints a rising score. The CompareTag check (rather than other.tag ==) avoids a string allocation and is the recommended idiom.

Use GetAxisRaw for snappy control.
GetAxis smooths input over time (good for a plane's throttle); GetAxisRaw returns hard -1/0/1 for instant response — usually what you want for a tight-feeling character.
🎮 Build This: Roll-a-Ball (collect)

A physics ball that rolls through pickups and scores.

  1. Add a Rigidbody to the Player; drive it with AddForce in FixedUpdate.
  2. Create a Pickup tag and several small cubes with Is Trigger enabled.
  3. In OnTriggerEnter, check the tag, deactivate the pickup, and increment score.
  4. Print the score with Debug.Log and confirm pickups vanish on contact.

title: Prefabs & 2D Essentials
html:
Prefabs: reusable GameObject blueprints

A Prefab is a saved GameObject (with all its components and children) stored as an asset. You can drop many copies into scenes or spawn them at runtime, and editing the prefab asset updates every instance. They're how you avoid hand-placing identical objects and how you spawn things in code.

Make a prefab

  1. Build the object you want once (a platform, an enemy, a coin).
  2. Drag it from the Hierarchy into the Project window. It turns blue in the Hierarchy — that means "this is now a prefab instance."
  3. Double-click the prefab asset to enter Prefab Mode and edit the master. Changes propagate to all instances.
Overrides are per-instance.
Change a value on one instance and it shows in bold with a blue line; that's an override that won't be touched by prefab edits. Use the Overrides dropdown to Apply (push to prefab) or Revert.

Spawn prefabs in code

PlatformSpawner.cs
using UnityEngine; public class PlatformSpawner : MonoBehaviour { [SerializeField] private GameObject platformPrefab; [SerializeField] private int count = 8; [SerializeField] private float gap = 2.5f; void Start() { for (int i = 0; i < count; i++) { Vector3 pos = new Vector3(i * gap, Random.Range(-1f, 2f), 0f); // Instantiate(prefab, position, rotation) Instantiate(platformPrefab, pos, Quaternion.identity); } } }

Drag your platform prefab asset into the Platform Prefab slot in the Inspector. Now Instantiate creates fresh copies at runtime.

Switching to 2D

2D in Unity is the same engine viewed down the Z axis with 2D-specific components. Click the 2D button in the Scene view to lock to an orthographic top-down editing view. The key swaps:

3D2D equivalent
Mesh RendererSprite Renderer
RigidbodyRigidbody2D
Box/Sphere ColliderBoxCollider2D / CircleCollider2D
Camera (Perspective)Camera (Orthographic)

A sprite player with run and jump

Import a PNG (drag it into Project), set its Texture Type to Sprite (2D and UI) in the Inspector, then drag it into the scene to create a Sprite Renderer GameObject. Add a Rigidbody2D and a BoxCollider2D.

Platformer2D.cs
using UnityEngine; [RequireComponent(typeof(Rigidbody2D))] public class Platformer2D : MonoBehaviour { [SerializeField] private float moveSpeed = 6f; [SerializeField] private float jumpForce = 9f; [SerializeField] private LayerMask groundLayer; [SerializeField] private Transform groundCheck; private Rigidbody2D rb; void Awake() => rb = GetComponent<Rigidbody2D>(); void Update() { float x = Input.GetAxisRaw("Horizontal"); rb.velocity = new Vector2(x * moveSpeed, rb.velocity.y); bool grounded = Physics2D.OverlapCircle( groundCheck.position, 0.1f, groundLayer); if (grounded && Input.GetButtonDown("Jump")) rb.velocity = new Vector2(rb.velocity.x, jumpForce); } }
Don't jump without a ground check.
If you let Jump fire whenever Space is pressed, the player double/triple-jumps midair. The OverlapCircle against a Ground layer at the character's feet is the standard fix — put an empty child groundCheck at the sprite's base and assign your platform's layer to groundLayer.
Keep gravity on the Rigidbody2D.
Set Gravity Scale to ~3 for a snappy platformer feel rather than the floaty default of 1, and freeze Rotation Z in the Rigidbody2D constraints so the player doesn't tip over.
🎮 Build This: 2D Platformer Base

A sprite player that runs and jumps across spawned prefab platforms.

  1. Switch Scene to 2D; import a sprite and add Sprite Renderer + Rigidbody2D + BoxCollider2D.
  2. Write Platformer2D with velocity-based move and a ground-checked jump.
  3. Make one platform, turn it into a prefab, set its layer to Ground.
  4. Write PlatformSpawner and Instantiate a row of platforms at Start.
  5. Assign the groundCheck child and groundLayer; press Play and traverse the level.

title: The New Input System
html:
Why replace Input.GetAxis

The old Input class hard-codes keys and doesn't cleanly support multiple gamepads, rebinding, or touch. The modern Input System package decouples actions ("Move", "Jump", "Dash") from bindings (WASD, left stick, a button), so the same gameplay code works across keyboard, gamepad, and controller without changes.

Install and enable

  1. Window → Package Manager → Unity Registry → install Input System.
  2. It prompts to enable the new backend and restart the Editor — accept it. (You can also set Edit → Project Settings → Player → Active Input Handling to Both during migration.)

Create an Input Actions asset

  1. Project window → right-click → Create → Input Actions. Name it PlayerControls.
  2. Double-click to open the editor. Add an Action Map called Gameplay.
  3. Add actions: Move (Action Type: Value, Control Type: Vector2), Jump (Button), Dash (Button).
  4. For Move, add a 2D Vector Composite binding and wire Up/Down/Left/Right to W/S/A/D, plus a second binding to the gamepad's Left Stick. For Jump bind Space and gamepad South button. For Dash bind Left Shift and gamepad West button.
  5. Check Generate C# Class at the top of the asset's Inspector and Apply — this creates a typed wrapper class.
PlayerInputHandler.cs
using UnityEngine; using UnityEngine.InputSystem; public class PlayerInputHandler : MonoBehaviour { private PlayerControls controls; // generated class private Vector2 moveInput; private Rigidbody2D rb; [SerializeField] private float moveSpeed = 6f; [SerializeField] private float jumpForce = 9f; [SerializeField] private float dashForce = 18f; void Awake() { rb = GetComponent<Rigidbody2D>(); controls = new PlayerControls(); // Event callbacks fire on press, not polled controls.Gameplay.Jump.performed += OnJump; controls.Gameplay.Dash.performed += OnDash; } void OnEnable() => controls.Gameplay.Enable(); void OnDisable() => controls.Gameplay.Disable(); void Update() { // Value actions are polled each frame moveInput = controls.Gameplay.Move.ReadValue<Vector2>(); rb.velocity = new Vector2(moveInput.x * moveSpeed, rb.velocity.y); } void OnJump(InputAction.CallbackContext ctx) { rb.velocity = new Vector2(rb.velocity.x, jumpForce); } void OnDash(InputAction.CallbackContext ctx) { float dir = Mathf.Sign(moveInput.x == 0 ? 1 : moveInput.x); rb.AddForce(new Vector2(dir * dashForce, 0f), ForceMode2D.Impulse); } }
Enable and disable maps with the object.
Always call Enable() in OnEnable and Disable() in OnDisable. Forgetting to enable the map is why "nothing happens" — the actions exist but aren't listening. Disabling on teardown prevents leaked callbacks.

Value vs. Button actions

Action typeHow you read it
Value (e.g. Move)Poll with ReadValue<T>() in Update — it has a continuous state.
Button (Jump, Dash)Subscribe to .performed — fires once on press. Also .started / .canceled.
Test devices instantly.
The Input Actions editor and the Input Debugger (Window → Analysis → Input Debugger) show live device input. Plug in a controller and watch the left stick feed the Move action — no code needed to verify bindings.
Don't mix old and new carelessly.
If Active Input Handling is set to the new system only, any leftover Input.GetAxis call throws an exception at runtime. Either migrate fully or set the handler to Both while transitioning.
🎮 Build This: Platformer with Actions

Replace direct key reads with Input System actions for move, jump, and dash.

  1. Install the Input System package and enable the new backend.
  2. Create a PlayerControls asset with Move (Vector2), Jump, and Dash; bind keyboard and gamepad.
  3. Enable Generate C# Class and Apply.
  4. Write PlayerInputHandler: poll Move in Update, subscribe Jump/Dash to .performed.
  5. Enable/Disable the map in OnEnable/OnDisable; test with both keyboard and a controller.

title: UI with Canvas & uGUI
html:
Everything UI lives on a Canvas

uGUI (Unity's built-in UI) draws all elements as children of a Canvas. Add one via GameObject → UI → Canvas; Unity also auto-creates an EventSystem (required for buttons to receive clicks). A Canvas has a Render Mode:

Render ModeUse
Screen Space - OverlayDefault. Draws on top of everything, ignores cameras. HUDs, menus.
Screen Space - CameraRendered by a camera; can sit at a distance and respect perspective.
World SpaceThe canvas is a physical object in the scene (e.g. a sign, a health bar over an enemy).

Make the Canvas resolution-independent

Select the Canvas → on the Canvas Scaler component set UI Scale Mode to Scale With Screen Size and a reference resolution like 1920×1080. Without this, UI is sized in raw pixels and breaks on other screens.

Anchors: the thing everyone gets wrong

Every UI element has a RectTransform with anchors — points it stays pinned to relative to its parent. Anchor a score label to the top-left corner and it stays in the corner on any resolution; leave it centered and it drifts. Use the anchor presets widget (top-left of the RectTransform), and hold Alt while clicking a preset to also set position.

Anchor to the nearest edge.
Top-left HUD → anchor top-left. Centered menu → anchor center. A bar that should stretch full-width → set left/right anchors apart so it grows with the screen. Get anchors right and your UI just works on every device.

Wire UI to code

Add a TextMeshPro text for the score (UI → Text - TextMeshPro; import TMP Essentials when prompted — TMP is sharper than legacy Text). Add a Button - TextMeshPro for Restart. Then drive them from a manager.

RunnerUI.cs
using UnityEngine; using UnityEngine.UI; using TMPro; public class RunnerUI : MonoBehaviour { [SerializeField] private TMP_Text scoreText; [SerializeField] private GameObject gameOverPanel; [SerializeField] private Button restartButton; private float distance; private bool dead; void Start() { gameOverPanel.SetActive(false); // Hook the button click in code (or via Inspector) restartButton.onClick.AddListener(Restart); } void Update() { if (dead) return; distance += Time.deltaTime * 10f; scoreText.text = $"Score: {Mathf.FloorToInt(distance)}"; } public void GameOver() { dead = true; gameOverPanel.SetActive(true); } void Restart() { UnityEngine.SceneManagement.SceneManager.LoadScene( UnityEngine.SceneManagement.SceneManager.GetActiveScene().buildIndex); } }

Drag the score Text, the game-over Panel, and the Button into the matching Inspector slots. You can wire onClick either in code (shown above) or by clicking + on the Button's On Click () list and selecting a public method — both are valid; code-wiring survives prefab refactors better.

The endless runner

An auto-runner is the perfect UI showcase: the player moves on its own, obstacles spawn, and the only inputs are jump and the restart button. Reuse last lesson's player but give it constant forward velocity. Spawn obstacle prefabs on a timer ahead of the player; when an obstacle's trigger overlaps the player, call RunnerUI.GameOver().

No EventSystem = dead buttons.
If clicking a Button does nothing, check the Hierarchy for an EventSystem object. Deleting it (or having a fullscreen invisible Image with Raycast Target on top) silently eats every click.
Pause time on game over.
Set Time.timeScale = 0f to freeze gameplay behind the panel, and reset it to 1f on restart. Remember UI animations using unscaled time still run.
🎮 Build This: Endless Runner

An auto-running player with spawned obstacles, live score, and a game-over screen.

  1. Give the player constant forward velocity and a jump.
  2. Spawn obstacle prefabs on a timer in front of the player; despawn them behind it.
  3. Add a Canvas (Scale With Screen Size); place a top-anchored TMP score and a centered game-over Panel + Restart button.
  4. Write RunnerUI: increment score in Update, show the panel on death, wire Restart to reload the scene.
  5. On obstacle-trigger collision, call GameOver and freeze with Time.timeScale.

title: Animation with the Animator
html:
Clips, Controllers, and the state machine

Unity animation has three layers. Animation Clips are recorded property changes over time (a run cycle, a muzzle flash). An Animator Controller is a state machine that decides which clip plays and how states transition. The Animator component on a GameObject runs a Controller. You drive transitions with parameters your code sets.

Create clips

  1. Select your character. Open Window → Animation → Animation. Click Create and save a clip named Idle.
  2. For sprite animation, drag a sequence of sprites onto the timeline to make a frame-by-frame clip; for property animation, hit record and change values at different keyframes.
  3. Make Run and Shoot clips the same way. Set Idle and Run to Loop Time in their import settings; leave Shoot non-looping.

Build the state machine

Open Window → Animation → Animator. You'll see your clips as states with Entry → Idle as default. Add parameters (the + in the Parameters panel):

ParameterTypeDrives
SpeedFloatIdle ↔ Run
ShootTriggerAny → Shoot (one-shot)

Right-click Idle → Make Transition → click Run. Select the arrow and add a Condition: Speed > 0.1. Make the reverse transition Run → Idle with Speed < 0.1. For shooting, use a transition from Any State → Shoot with condition Shoot (trigger), and Shoot → Idle with Has Exit Time checked so it returns when the clip finishes.

Turn off Has Exit Time for responsive moves.
By default a transition waits for the current clip to finish (Exit Time). For instant Idle↔Run swaps, uncheck Has Exit Time and set transition duration low (~0.05). Keep it on for fire-and-finish animations like Shoot.

Drive it from code

ShooterCharacter.cs
using UnityEngine; public class ShooterCharacter : MonoBehaviour { [SerializeField] private float moveSpeed = 5f; [SerializeField] private GameObject bulletPrefab; [SerializeField] private Transform muzzle; private Animator anim; private Rigidbody2D rb; // Cache parameter hashes — faster than string lookups private static readonly int SpeedHash = Animator.StringToHash("Speed"); private static readonly int ShootHash = Animator.StringToHash("Shoot"); void Awake() { anim = GetComponent<Animator>(); rb = GetComponent<Rigidbody2D>(); } void Update() { Vector2 move = new Vector2( Input.GetAxisRaw("Horizontal"), Input.GetAxisRaw("Vertical")); rb.velocity = move * moveSpeed; anim.SetFloat(SpeedHash, move.sqrMagnitude); // 0 when idle if (Input.GetButtonDown("Fire1")) { anim.SetTrigger(ShootHash); Instantiate(bulletPrefab, muzzle.position, muzzle.rotation); } } }
Hash your parameter names once.
SetFloat("Speed", x) hashes the string every call. Animator.StringToHash in a static readonly field does it once. With many animated characters this is real savings — and it's the pro idiom.
Triggers can stick.
A Trigger stays "set" until a transition consumes it. If your Any State → Shoot transition condition is wrong, the trigger queues and fires later unexpectedly. Make sure exactly one transition consumes the trigger, and avoid setting it every frame.
Use sqrMagnitude for the Speed check.
Comparing move.sqrMagnitude avoids a square root and works fine since you only test against a threshold. Idle reads ~0; moving reads >0.
🎮 Build This: Top-Down Shooter Core

A character that animates idle/run/shoot and fires prefab bullets.

  1. Make Idle, Run (looping), and Shoot (one-shot) clips for your sprite.
  2. In the Animator add a Float Speed and a Trigger Shoot; wire Idle↔Run and Any→Shoot transitions.
  3. Create a bullet prefab with a Rigidbody2D and a script that moves it forward.
  4. Write ShooterCharacter: set Speed from movement, SetTrigger on fire, and Instantiate a bullet at a muzzle Transform.
  5. Tune transition Has Exit Time so Run is snappy and Shoot finishes its clip.

title: Capstone: Scenes, Build Settings & Shipping
html:
Multiple scenes, one game

Real games split into scenes: a Main Menu, the gameplay level(s), maybe a separate Game Over. Each scene is a saved file. You move between them at runtime with the SceneManager, and you must register every scene that ships in Build Settings or it won't be included.

Set up scene flow

  1. Create MainMenu and Game scenes (File → New Scene, then Save As into Assets/Scenes).
  2. Open File → Build Settings. Drag both scenes into Scenes In Build. The order matters: index 0 is what loads first — put MainMenu on top.
MenuManager.cs
using UnityEngine; using UnityEngine.SceneManagement; public class MenuManager : MonoBehaviour { // Wire these to button On Click() events public void PlayGame() { SceneManager.LoadScene("Game"); } public void QuitGame() { Application.Quit(); Debug.Log("Quit (no effect in the Editor)"); } }

On your menu, add a Play Button whose On Click() calls MenuManager.PlayGame, and a Quit Button calling QuitGame. Reference scenes by name (as above) or build index — name is more readable.

If it's not in Build Settings, it doesn't exist.
LoadScene("Game") works in the Editor only if the scene is registered. A standalone build that omits a scene throws "Scene couldn't be loaded." Always confirm the Scenes In Build list before building.

Persisting data across scenes

Loading a scene destroys the previous one's GameObjects. To carry a score or settings across scenes, mark a manager with DontDestroyOnLoad(gameObject) in its Awake, or store values in PlayerPrefs for simple persistence between sessions.

GameManager.cs
using UnityEngine; public class GameManager : MonoBehaviour { public static GameManager Instance; void Awake() { // Singleton that survives scene loads if (Instance != null) { Destroy(gameObject); return; } Instance = this; DontDestroyOnLoad(gameObject); } public int HighScore { get => PlayerPrefs.GetInt("HighScore", 0); set => PlayerPrefs.SetInt("HighScore", value); } }

Producing a desktop build

  1. File → Build Settings → choose your platform (Windows / Mac / Linux) and click Switch Platform if needed.
  2. Open Player Settings: set Company Name, Product Name, an icon, and the default resolution / fullscreen mode.
  3. Click Build, pick an empty output folder, and wait. Unity produces an executable plus a _Data folder (Windows) or an .app bundle (Mac).
  4. Use Build And Run to launch it immediately and confirm it works outside the Editor.
The Editor lies about some things.
Things that work in Play mode can break in a build: Application.Quit does nothing in the Editor, files in Assets may not exist at the same path, and Debug.Log noise costs performance. Always test the actual build before calling it done.
Strip and compress for release.
In Player Settings, set the scripting backend appropriately, enable managed stripping, and check texture compression. A debug build is fine for testing; flip to a release configuration before sharing.

Your capstone checklist

Combine everything: a MainMenu scene with Play/Quit, a Game scene using physics or 2D movement, an Animator-driven character, a uGUI HUD with live score and a game-over flow, and a persistent high score. Then ship a standalone build.

🎮 Build This: Capstone Game + Build

A complete small Unity game with menu and gameplay scenes, animation, UI, and a desktop build.

  1. Create MainMenu and Game scenes; add both to Build Settings with MainMenu at index 0.
  2. Wire a Play button to SceneManager.LoadScene("Game") and a Quit button to Application.Quit.
  3. Build gameplay reusing earlier skills: input-driven movement, an Animator character, physics/triggers, and a uGUI HUD.
  4. Add a GameManager with DontDestroyOnLoad + PlayerPrefs to persist a high score.
  5. Set Player Settings (name, icon, resolution); use Build And Run to produce and launch a desktop build, then test it outside the Editor.
